hook: You already know how to represent relationships between objects using graphs, where vertices are connected by edges. Now imagine you are a hospital administrator trying to assign doctors to shifts: each doctor has availability constraints, and each shift needs exactly one doctor. You can draw this as a graph, but how do you know a valid assignment even exists, let alone find one efficiently? The key question is: when can we perfectly pair up two groups of objects with no conflicts?
concept: A bipartite graph is a graph whose vertices can be split into two disjoint sets so that every edge connects a vertex in one set to a vertex in the other — never two vertices within the same set. Formally, a graph $G = (V, E)$ is bipartite if there exists a partition $V = A \cup B$ with $A \cap B = \emptyset$ such that every edge $e \in E$ has one endpoint in $A$ and one endpoint in $B$. A matching $M \subseteq E$ is a set of edges with no shared endpoints, and a perfect matching covers every vertex.
anatomy: A bipartite graph has several key components worth naming precisely. The two vertex classes $A$ and $B$ (sometimes called the left and right partitions) contain the two groups being matched. Each edge $(a, b)$ with $a \in A$, $b \in B$ represents a compatible or allowable pairing. A matching $M$ is the subset of chosen edges where every vertex appears at most once; if $|M| = |A| = |B|$, we call it a perfect matching. Hall's condition is the structural criterion $|N(S)| \geq |S|$ for all $S \subseteq A$, where $N(S)$ denotes the neighbourhood of $S$ — the set of all vertices in $B$ adjacent to at least one vertex of $S$.

worked_example.description_latex: We start by clearly identifying the two vertex classes. Here $A$ represents three job applicants and $B$ represents three job positions, so every edge we draw will go between an applicant and a position — never between two applicants or two positions.

These edges encode which applicants are qualified for which positions. Before looking for a matching, we should verify the graph is bipartite — since every edge runs between $A$ and $B$, this is satisfied automatically by construction.

To check Hall's condition, we must verify that for every subset $S \subseteq A$, the neighbourhood $N(S)$ is at least as large as $S$. We begin with the singleton subsets; $a_1$ is connected to both $b_1$ and $b_2$, so this subset passes easily.

We must also check all larger subsets, including the full set $A$ itself. Since collectively the three applicants cover all three positions, Hall's condition holds for this subset too — and after checking every subset, Hall's theorem guarantees a perfect matching exists.

We exhibit an explicit perfect matching: $a_1$ gets position $b_2$, $a_2$ gets $b_3$, and $a_3$ gets $b_1$. You can confirm this is a valid matching because no vertex appears more than once, and every vertex in both $A$ and $B$ is covered.

partial_example.description_latex: We are given this bipartite graph and asked whether Hall's condition holds for all subsets of $A$. We have $|A| = 2$ and $|B| = 3$, so we need to check the three non-empty subsets of $A$: the two singletons and the full set.

partial_example.expression_latex: S = \{p\} \Rightarrow N(S) = \{x, y\},\quad S = \{q\} \Rightarrow N(S) = \{y, z\}
partial_example.description_latex: The two singleton subsets both satisfy $|N(S)| \geq |S|$ since each applicant is connected to two positions. Now you need to check the remaining non-trivial subset and state your conclusion about whether a perfect matching of $A$ into $B$ exists.

Check Hall's condition for $S = \{p, q\}$ by finding $N(\{p,q\})$, then decide: does a perfect matching (saturating all of $A$) exist? Write $N(\{p,q\})$, verify the inequality, and if it holds, state one valid matching $M$.

Since $N(\{p,q\}) = \{x,y,z\}$ and $|N(\{p,q\})| = 3 \geq 2 = |\{p,q\}|$, Hall's condition is satisfied for every subset, so a perfect matching of $A$ into $B$ exists. One valid matching is $(p,x)$ paired with $(q,z)$ — note that $y$ is left unmatched in $B$, which is fine since $|B| > |A|$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Is the cycle graph $C_4$ (a four-cycle) bipartite? Justify your answer by finding a valid 2-colouring or explaining why none exists.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Yes. Partition the four vertices as $A = \{v_1, v_3\}$ and $B = \{v_2, v_4\}$; all edges run between $A$ and $B$, confirming $C_4$ is bipartite.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $A = \{a_1, a_2\}$, $B = \{b_1, b_2\}$, and $E = \{(a_1,b_1),(a_2,b_1)\}$. Check Hall's condition for every non-empty $S \subseteq A$ and state whether a perfect matching exists.
practice_problems.answer_latex: $N(\{a_1\}) = \{b_1\}$, $N(\{a_2\}) = \{b_1\}$, $N(\{a_1,a_2\}) = \{b_1\}$. Hall's condition fails for $S = \{a_1,a_2\}$ since $|N(S)| = 1 < 2 = |S|$, so no perfect matching exists.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: For the bipartite graph with $A = \{1,2,3\}$, $B = \{x,y,z\}$, and edges $\{(1,x),(1,y),(2,y),(2,z),(3,x),(3,z)\}$, verify Hall's condition holds for all subsets of $A$ and exhibit a perfect matching.
practice_problems.answer_latex: All seven non-empty subsets of $A$ satisfy $|N(S)| \geq |S|$. One perfect matching is $M = \{(1,y),(2,z),(3,x)\}$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A graph $G$ is bipartite if and only if it contains no odd-length cycle. Use this theorem to determine whether the complete graph $K_3$ is bipartite, and state the size of the largest matching in $K_3$.
practice_problems.answer_latex: $K_3$ contains the 3-cycle $v_1 v_2 v_3$, which has odd length, so $K_3$ is not bipartite. The largest matching in $K_3$ has size 1 (any single edge, since no two edges in $K_3$ are independent).

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Five students $\{S_1,S_2,S_3,S_4,S_5\}$ must each be assigned to one of five project topics $\{T_1,T_2,T_3,T_4,T_5\}$. Their preferences form a bipartite graph where $S_1 \to \{T_1,T_2\}$, $S_2 \to \{T_2,T_3\}$, $S_3 \to \{T_1,T_4\}$, $S_4 \to \{T_3,T_5\}$, $S_5 \to \{T_4,T_5\}$. Does a perfect assignment exist? If so, give one.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Check Hall's condition: every subset $S$ of students has $|N(S)| \geq |S|$ (verify key cases such as $S=\{S_1,S_2\}$ giving $N=\{T_1,T_2,T_3\}$). A perfect matching is $\{(S_1,T_2),(S_2,T_3),(S_3,T_1),(S_4,T_5),(S_5,T_4)\}$.

common_mistakes: Students often check Hall's condition only for the singleton subsets $\{a\}$ and the full set $A$, skipping intermediate subsets. The condition must hold for every non-empty $S \subseteq A$, and a violation can occur at any subset size — always enumerate all $2^{|A|}-1$ non-empty subsets for small graphs.

When a perfect matching is requested but $|A| \neq |B|$, students sometimes think no matching is possible. A perfect matching in a bipartite graph means every vertex of $A$ is matched (saturated), even if vertices in $B$ are left unmatched — Hall's condition only requires $|N(S)| \geq |S|$ for subsets of $A$.

Students confuse 'the graph has a matching of size $k$' with 'the graph is bipartite.' Bipartiteness is a structural property about the partition of vertices, not about the existence or size of any particular matching; a non-bipartite graph can still have large matchings.
untested_variants: Finding a maximum matching (not necessarily perfect) using the augmenting path algorithm or König's theorem in a bipartite graph where Hall's condition fails.
Proving or applying König's theorem, which states that in any bipartite graph the size of the maximum matching equals the size of the minimum vertex cover.
Determining whether a given graph is bipartite using breadth-first search and 2-colouring, then reporting the partition sets explicitly.
Counting the total number of distinct perfect matchings in a bipartite graph, which connects to the permanent of the biadjacency matrix.
